FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study (baseline N~12,000; Mage=9.9 years; recruited from 21 U.S. sites; ABCD 5.1 release)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using parallel process latent growth curve models to examine associations between social media use and cognitive performance trajectories across 4 years

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article (Journal of Research on Adolescence, 2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Variable label is "social media use" (phone/SM-specific item per step 1 search). Longitudinal analysis: trajectory of social media use paired with crystallized abilities and inhibitory control/attention (individual-trait-level cognitive constructs). Social media use appears as IV in the model predicting cognitive performance trajectories.

FT_IC3.evidence: Latent growth curve parameter estimates reported including slopes and slope-slope covariances with standard errors, confidence intervals, and p-values (Table 3, Model 5: iSM→sCryst β=-0.04, p=0.024; Table 2 shows intercepts and slopes with p<0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results for SM-cognition relationships: parallel process model shows significant cross-construct associations between social media hours and cognitive performance trajectories with reported path coefficients and p-values

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design present; however, SM is IV (predicts cognitive trajectories) not DV-only, so EC7 carve-out does not apply
notes: Bidirectional associations tested using RI-CLPM; study uses parallel process latent growth curve models separating within-person effects from between-person effects

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from U.S. ABCD Study (N=8,699 at T1; ages 9-12 at baseline) with ABCD-specific estimates extractable from target trial emulation analyses; also includes Generation R data but ABCD analysis is primary focus

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using target trial emulation (TTE) and G-formula to estimate potential causal effects of modifiable factors on internalizing symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article (Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Variable label is "screen time (ST)" which is multi-device aggregated (TV shows or movies, watching videos, playing videogames, texting, visiting social networking sites, video chatting). Per v7 binding rule, multi-device aggregated time without per-device breakout fails IC2. While OSA (texting + social media + video chatting) is isolated in methods, primary analyses use ST composite including non-phone items (TV, movies, unspecified gaming, streaming video). Step 2: Composite includes non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, movies, generic video streaming) bundled without separate breakout in main ST analyses → NOT_MET

notes: Although paper measures OSA separately from TV/gaming, main analyses focus on ST composite. OSA is noted in methods but primary intervention targeting and outcome analyses center on multi-device screen time

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study (N=11,819 at baseline; Mage=9.92 years; SD=0.62; 21 U.S. recruitment sites; ABCD 5.0 release)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using random-intercepts cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPMs) to examine bidirectional longitudinal associations across 4 waves of data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article (European Child & Adolescent Psychiatry, 2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Variable label is "Online social activity (OSA) time" - phone/SM-specific composite measured as sum of time on "texting", "visiting social networking sites", and "video chatting". Paired with ADHD problems (individual-trait-level behavioral/psychiatric outcome). OSA appears as IV in cross-lagged model predicting ADHD problems within-person. Meets Step 1 criteria → MET

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-lagged coefficients reported: OSA time T2→T3 ADHD β=0.094 (SE=0.041, p=0.023) for girls; β=-0.001 (SE=0.030, p=0.967) for boys; OSA time T3→T4 ADHD β=0.080 (SE=0.028, p=0.005) for girls; β=0.007 (SE=0.036, p=0.844) for boys (Table 3)

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results for OSA-ADHD relationship including standardized path coefficients, standard errors, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values across time lags and by sex

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design present; however, SM/OSA is IV in cross-lagged model (bidirectional relationships tested with OSA as both IV and DV) so EC7 carve-out condition 3 (SM-as-IV analysis present) excludes this criterion
notes: Bidirectional associations tested; moderation by sex and OSA content type examined; RI-CLPM design isolates within-person effects from stable between-person effects
